nodes.title:
A team agreement only works if it’s alive in our daily habits. Writing rules is not enough — what truly brings them to life are rituals (regular practices), techniques (ways of working), and ideas (small experiments).


In this section we are brainstorming with the help of Psychological Safety Coach on possible ideas how to live this agreement day to day.

A team agreement is a set of shared commitments that help us define how we want to work together. It clarifies expectations, reduces misunderstandings, and builds trust.


When we co-create our agreements, everyone has a voice in shaping the way we collaborate. The agreement is not static, we can review and update it as our team evolves.

1. Create shared and clear expectations.
  2. Strengthen trust, collaboration, and psychological safety.
  3. Give every team member the opportunity to shape how we work.
  4. End with a document that everyone signs as a symbol of commitment.

When writing our rules, keep in mind:

  1. Use “we” statements (e.g., We agree to…).
  2. Be concrete (e.g., We reply to messages within 24h instead of We communicate regularly).
  3. Positive framing (e.g., We respect each other’s opinions instead of We don’t interrupt).
  4. Keep it short and clear – simpler is better.
  5. Think about our values

For each area, we will brainstorm potential rules, then vote to select the most important ones. Example areas:

  1. Communication (response time, transparency).
  2. Meetings (how we prepare, how we ensure efficiency).
  3. Conflict resolution (how we handle disagreements).
  4. Support & collaboration (how we help each other, how we celebrate wins).
  5. Learning & feedback (how we give feedback, how we learn together).

Team Agreement

Introduction

This Team Agreement represents our collective commitment to how we want to work together. It clarifies our shared expectations, establishes clear communication channels, and creates a foundation of trust and mutual respect. By co-creating these guidelines, we ensure that everyone has a voice in shaping our collaborative environment. This agreement is a living document that we can revisit and refine as our team evolves and grows together.

Our Commitments

  1. We communicate transparently and proactively, keeping everyone informed of updates, sharing useful resources openly with the team, and telling teammates when we are offline or unavailable.
  2. We respect each other's time by starting and ending meetings on time, keeping meetings under 60 minutes, and sharing meeting agendas at least 24 hours in advance.
  3. We address issues directly and constructively by speaking with the person involved first, raising conflicts within 48 hours rather than letting them fester, and using "I" statements instead of blame.
  4. We support each other's growth by treating mistakes as learning opportunities, sharing mistakes openly in retrospectives, and using retrospectives to suggest improvements.
  5. We ensure inclusive participation by giving everyone equal time to speak, listening fully before responding, and checking understanding by paraphrasing before moving on.
  6. We maintain clear and accessible communication by using Slack for quick updates, email for formal information, and using clear, simple language without jargon.
  7. We respect work-life boundaries by not sending work messages after 6pm and respecting "no meeting" focus hours.
  8. We collaborate effectively by seeking win-win solutions rather than just compromises, pairing up for complex tasks, and flagging blockers as soon as they appear.
  9. We offer mutual support by checking in on each other's workload, offering help when someone is overloaded, and covering for teammates when they're absent.
  10. We provide constructive feedback by keeping it specific and actionable, giving feedback immediately and one-to-one, and asking for feedback after completing tasks.

Commitment Statement

With my signature, I fully agree to commit to this agreement, recognizing that it represents our shared understanding of how we want to work together as a team. I acknowledge that these commitments are designed to foster trust, enhance collaboration, and create a positive and productive work environment. I will strive to uphold these principles in my daily interactions with my teammates, and I understand that we may revisit and refine this agreement as our team evolves. By signing below, I demonstrate my personal commitment to contributing to our team's success through these collaborative practices.

Signature

Name (Printed)

Date

They:

  1. Make our agreements visible in action, not just on paper.
  2. Help us practice consistency — agreements turn into habits.
  3. Create shared reminders of what we value as a team.
  4. Show how we are already living some commitments today.
  5. Give us space to experiment with new practices that strengthen our culture.

Let's take a look at our agreement & write on stickies what we should :

  1. Continue doing: What are we already doing that shows we are living our agreement? Which practices, habits, or behaviors are worth continuing?
  2. Add: What rituals, ideas, or techniques could we add to make our agreement stronger? These don’t need to be big changes — small, regular practices can make the biggest difference.
  3. Remove: Which habits, routines, or behaviors do we need to stop because they go against our agreement or weaken it?
Sending late-night or weekend work messages without agreement.
Gossiping or escalating issues before talking directly to the person involved.
Ignoring action items we’ve committed to.
Treating the agreement as a static document instead of a living one.
A quick ‘win of the week’ round at Friday check-ins.
A kudos wall or shout-out board for appreciation.
A quarterly check-in on the agreement itself: are we still living it?
A short “learning bite” in weekly meetings to share insights.
We already start most meetings on time.
We often celebrate small wins and successes.
We keep each other updated on Slack.
We usually go directly to the person when there’s an issue.

Red/Yellow/Green Check-in

Start meetings with a quick emotional temperature check. Each person shares their current state (red=struggling, yellow=cautious, green=good) and one sentence why. Creates space for honesty and helps others adjust their communication approach accordingly.

Appreciation Circle

End meetings with 2 minutes where team members acknowledge specific contributions. "I appreciated when you..." format focuses on behaviors not personalities. Reinforces psychological safety and builds trust through regular positive recognition.

Conflict Resolution Protocol

When disagreements arise, use the "I noticed... I felt... I need..." framework. This structured approach prevents blame, focuses on impact, and creates clear paths forward. Practice in low-stakes situations before applying to bigger conflicts.

Learning Retrospective

Weekly 15-minute session where team members share one thing they learned, one challenge they faced, and one way others could help. Creates regular feedback loops and normalizes both success and struggle as part of growth.

Agreement Guardian Role

Rotate a weekly role where one team member gently reminds the team of agreements when they notice drift. Uses a predetermined phrase like "I'd like to revisit our agreement about..." Distributes responsibility for upholding agreements across the team.
Implement a Red/Yellow/Green Check-in at the start of meetings to gauge emotional temperature.
End meetings with an Appreciation Circle to acknowledge specific contributions.
Use a Conflict Resolution Protocol with 'I noticed... I felt... I need...' framework.
Conduct a weekly Learning Retrospective to share learnings and challenges.
Rotate an Agreement Guardian Role to remind the team of agreements.
Introduce a 'Win of the Week' round at Friday check-ins.
Rotate meeting roles such as facilitator and timekeeper.
Schedule a quarterly check-in on the team agreement to ensure it's being lived.
Create a kudos wall or shout-out board for appreciation.
Include a short 'learning bite' in weekly meetings to share insights.

Implementation Steps for Voted Ideas

Use a Conflict Resolution Protocol with 'I noticed... I felt... I need...' framework

  1. Create a simple one-page guide documenting the framework with examples
  2. Schedule a 30-minute team training session to introduce the protocol
  3. Practice using the framework in pairs during low-stakes role-play scenarios
  4. Designate specific meeting time for addressing conflicts using this structure
  5. Review effectiveness of the protocol quarterly and adjust as needed

Rotate an Agreement Guardian Role to remind the team of agreements

  1. Document the specific responsibilities and phrases for the Guardian role
  2. Create a rotation schedule ensuring each team member serves as Guardian
  3. Add a standing agenda item for the Guardian to provide brief observations
  4. Establish a handoff ritual between outgoing and incoming Guardians
  5. Collect feedback on the Guardian role's impact during team retrospectives

Implement a Red/Yellow/Green Check-in at the start of meetings to gauge emotional temperature

  1. Design a simple visual aid explaining the red/yellow/green states
  2. Add the check-in as the first agenda item for all team meetings
  3. Model vulnerability by having a team leader go first in initial sessions
  4. Establish a time limit of 30 seconds per person to keep check-ins concise
  5. Document patterns from check-ins to identify recurring team challenges

If you want instructions on the

techniques that the Sidekick provided, click

the Run button by the document!

Push Run for writing your own Team Agreement!
We use Slack for quick updates, email for formal info.
We tell the team when we are offline or unavailable.
We flag blockers as soon as they appear.
We summarize key decisions in writing.
We use clear, simple language (no jargon).
We prefer a quick call for urgent matters.
We don’t send work messages after 6pm.
We keep everyone informed, no hidden updates.
We address issues directly with the person first.
We use “I” statements instead of blame.
We involve a neutral teammate if needed.
We raise conflicts within 48h, not weeks later.
We seek win–win solutions, not just compromises.
We assume good intent before judging.
We thank teammates for being honest.
We ask for feedback after completing tasks.
We share mistakes openly in retrospectives.
We treat mistakes as learning opportunities.
We share learnings from trainings with the team.
We reflect as a team after every project.
We use retrospectives to suggest improvements.
We offer help when someone is overloaded.
We celebrate small wins in our chat.
We share useful resources openly with the team.
We give credit publicly for contributions.
We ask for help early, not last minute.
We cover for teammates when they’re absent.
We check in on each other’s workload.
We recognize effort, not just outcomes.
We pair up for complex tasks.
We share successes across teams, not just internally.
We start and end meetings on time.
We share the agenda at least 24h in advance.
We rotate the facilitator for recurring meetings.
We end every meeting with clear action items.
We keep cameras on during online meetings (unless agreed otherwise).
We cancel meetings without a clear purpose.
We ensure everyone has equal time to speak.
We share meeting notes immediately after.

Appreciation Circle

End meetings with 2 minutes where team members acknowledge specific contributions. "I appreciated when you..." format focuses on behaviors not personalities. Reinforces psychological safety and builds trust through regular positive recognition.
Implement a Red/Yellow/Green Check-in at the start of meetings to gauge emotional temperature.
Use a Conflict Resolution Protocol with 'I noticed... I felt... I need...' framework.
Rotate an Agreement Guardian Role to remind the team of agreements.
Rotate meeting roles such as facilitator and timekeeper.

Plan about 45 minutes to complete the first frame.


Agree on the focus areas with your team before the workshop.


Give everyone 3–5 minutes per area to write down their agreements.


Once that’s done, read them aloud, group similar ones together if needed, and vote using dots or the voting tool.


When you’re ready, just press Run — the team agreement will be generated automatically.

Plan about 20 minutes for the second frame.


If the team is already working together, keep the Continue frame; otherwise remove it before the workshop.


Give everyone 3–5 minutes to write ideas on how you can live your team agreement day to day.


Read them aloud, group similar ones if needed, then vote using dots or the voting tool.


Move the winning items into the box labeled Voted ideas.


When you’re ready, press Run—you’ll get a clear step-by-step implementation for the voted ideas.
